title: AI with Python
tagline: Connect to GPT, build chatbots, and add intelligence to your code.

lessons.title: Make Your First AI API Call

accomplishments: Build a working chatbot in Python
Create an AI assistant that answers questions

do.title: Set up OpenAI and make your first AI call
steps: Install OpenAI library: pip install openai
Get API key from platform.openai.com/api-keys
Create file: ai_practice.py
Copy the code below
Run it — you just talked to an AI!
do.code:
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()  # Uses OPENAI_API_KEY env var

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-3.5-turbo",
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": "Say hello and introduce yourself in one sentence."}
    ]
)

ai_message = response.choices[0].message.content
print("AI Response:", ai_message)
print("\nYour first AI API call worked!")
do.result: You just talked to an AI! The model read your message and generated a unique response.
do.proofRequired: your terminal showing a successful AI response

concepts.name: Large Language Models (LLMs)
concepts.definition: AI models trained on massive text to understand and generate human-like responses. GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, Llama are all LLMs.
concepts.analogy: A student who has read every book in every library. They can discuss any topic because they've seen so much text.

concepts.name: Prompts
concepts.definition: The instructions you give the AI. Better prompts = better responses. Types: direct questions, instructions, role-based ('You are a tutor'), few-shot (examples).
concepts.analogy: Ordering at a restaurant. 'Give me food' gets you something random. 'I want a medium-rare ribeye with mashed potatoes' gets exactly what you want.

concepts.name: Tokens
concepts.definition: Pieces of text the AI reads and generates. ~4 characters = 1 token. 1000 tokens ≈ 750 words. You pay per token.
concepts.analogy: Like phone minutes. Each word costs a tiny amount. More words = higher bill.

concepts.name: Chat Message Format
concepts.definition: Messages are a list of {role, content} objects. Roles: 'system' (sets behavior), 'user' (your messages), 'assistant' (AI responses). History enables conversation memory.
concepts.analogy: A script for a play. Each line has a character (role) and their dialogue (content).

concepts.name: Temperature
concepts.definition: Controls randomness (0.0-2.0). Low (0.1) = focused, deterministic. High (1.5) = creative, varied. Default 0.7 is a good balance.
concepts.analogy: A creativity dial. Turn it down for math answers, turn it up for poetry.

concepts.name: System Prompts
concepts.definition: The 'system' role message that shapes AI personality and behavior. 'You are a Python tutor' vs 'You are a pirate' produces completely different responses.
concepts.analogy: Giving an actor their character description before the scene. Same script, different performance.

understand.keyTakeaway: AI APIs let you add intelligence to any application. System prompts control behavior, messages enable conversation, tokens determine cost.

build.goal: Build a chatbot with conversation memory and custom personality

codeComparison.new:
messages = [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are a friendly Python tutor."}
]

while True:
    user_input = input("You: ")
    if user_input == 'quit': break
    messages.append({"role": "user", "content": user_input})
    
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-3.5-turbo",
        messages=messages
    )
    ai_reply = response.choices[0].message.content
    messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": ai_reply})
    print(f"AI: {ai_reply}")

build.hint: The key to conversation memory: keep ALL messages in the list. Each new message adds to the history.

Quiz the user and grade their answers
Save conversation history to JSON file

lessons.devFession: I spent $47 on OpenAI tokens in one day because I forgot to set max_tokens and my loop kept running. Always set limits. Always check your usage dashboard.

lessons.title: Build AI-Powered Tools

accomplishments: Build a text summarizer with multiple styles
Create a content generator for blogs, emails, and social media

do.title: Build a text summarizer
steps: Create a new file: ai_tools.py
Copy the code below
Run it with a long text — watch AI condense it!
Try different styles: bullet, paragraph, tweet, eli5
do.code:
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

def summarize(text, style='bullet'):
    styles = {
        'bullet': 'in 3-5 bullet points',
        'paragraph': 'in a single paragraph',
        'tweet': 'in a tweet (280 chars max)',
        'eli5': 'like I am 5 years old'
    }
    prompt = f"Summarize this {styles.get(style, 'concisely')}:\n\n{text}"
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model='gpt-3.5-turbo',
        messages=[
            {'role': 'system', 'content': 'You are an expert summarizer.'},
            {'role': 'user', 'content': prompt}
        ],
        temperature=0.5
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.content

article = '''Artificial intelligence has transformed how we interact with 
technology. From voice assistants to recommendation systems, AI is 
everywhere. Machine learning, a subset of AI, allows computers to 
learn from data without being explicitly programmed...'''

for style in ['bullet', 'tweet', 'eli5']:
    print(f'\n=== {style.upper()} ===\n{summarize(article, style)}')
do.result: Same text, 3 completely different summaries. That's the power of prompt engineering!
do.proofRequired: your summarizer producing at least 3 different style outputs

concepts.name: Prompt Engineering
concepts.definition: The art of crafting AI prompts for better results. Be specific, provide context, give examples, set constraints. Better prompts = dramatically better outputs.
concepts.analogy: Like giving directions — 'go north' is vague. 'Take I-90 East for 3 miles, exit at Oak Street' gets you there.

concepts.name: AI Text Summarization
concepts.definition: Condensing long text into key points. Different styles (bullets, paragraph, tweet) serve different purposes.
concepts.analogy: Like a movie trailer vs a review vs a tweet about the film. Same content, different formats.

concepts.name: Sentiment Analysis
concepts.definition: Detecting emotions in text: Positive, Negative, Neutral, Mixed. Used for reviews, social media monitoring, customer feedback.
concepts.analogy: Reading the room — is the crowd happy, angry, or indifferent?

understand.keyTakeaway: Each AI tool is just a well-crafted prompt wrapped in a function. Master prompt engineering and you can build anything.

build.goal: Build a multi-language translator and sentiment analyzer

codeComparison.new:
def translate(text, target_lang):
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model='gpt-3.5-turbo',
        messages=[{'role': 'system', 'content': f'Translate to {target_lang}. Return ONLY the translation.'},
                  {'role': 'user', 'content': text}]
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.content

def analyze_sentiment(text):
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model='gpt-3.5-turbo',
        messages=[{'role': 'system', 'content': 'Analyze sentiment. Return JSON: {"sentiment": "positive/negative/neutral", "confidence": 0.95, "reasoning": "..."}'},
                  {'role': 'user', 'content': text}],
        temperature=0.3
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.content

build.hint: Low temperature (0.3) for analysis tasks. Higher (0.8) for creative generation.

lessons.title: Work with Local AI Models

accomplishments: Run AI on YOUR computer — no API costs
Build a text classifier and entity extractor
Understand when to use local vs API models

do.title: Run your first local AI model
steps: Install: pip install transformers torch
Create local_ai.py
Copy the code below
Run it — first run downloads the model (1-2 min)
do.code:
from transformers import pipeline

print('Loading AI model...\n')
analyzer = pipeline('sentiment-analysis')

texts = [
    'I love learning Python!',
    'This is terrible and frustrating.',
    'The weather is okay today.'
]

for text in texts:
    result = analyzer(text)[0]
    emoji = '😊' if result['label'] == 'POSITIVE' else '😞'
    print(f'{emoji} "{text}"')
    print(f'   Sentiment: {result["label"]} ({result["score"]:.1%})\n')
do.result: AI running on YOUR machine! No API key, no internet needed, no cost per query. The model lives on your computer.
do.proofRequired: your terminal showing local sentiment analysis results

concepts.name: Local vs API Models
concepts.definition: Local: free, private, works offline, you control everything. API: latest models, faster for complex tasks, pay per use. Use local for simple tasks and privacy. Use API for complex reasoning.
concepts.analogy: Cooking at home (local) vs ordering delivery (API). Home cooking is free but limited. Delivery has more options but costs money.

concepts.name: Hugging Face
concepts.definition: Platform with 100,000+ pre-trained AI models. The 'GitHub of AI.' Free to use. Models for text, images, audio, code.
concepts.analogy: An app store for AI models. Browse, download, use — all free.

concepts.name: Transformers Pipelines
concepts.definition: Pre-built tools for common AI tasks: sentiment-analysis, ner (entity extraction), question-answering, summarization, translation. One line to load, one line to use.
concepts.analogy: Power tools. Instead of building from scratch, grab the right tool for the job.

understand.keyTakeaway: Local models = free, private, offline. API models = powerful, latest, scalable. Use both strategically.

build.goal: Build a text classifier and named entity extractor

codeComparison.old:
analyzer = pipeline('sentiment-analysis')
result = analyzer('I love Python!')[0]
codeComparison.new:
# Named Entity Recognition
ner = pipeline('ner', grouped_entities=True)
text = 'Jenna works at CodeHerWay in Chicago'
entities = ner(text)
for e in entities:
    print(f'{e["word"]}: {e["entity_group"]} ({e["score"]:.1%})')

# Question Answering
qa = pipeline('question-answering')
result = qa(question='Where does Jenna work?',
            context='Jenna works at CodeHerWay in Chicago.')
print(f'Answer: {result["answer"]} ({result["score"]:.1%})')

build.hint: First run downloads models (100-500MB). After that, they load from cache instantly.

lessons.devFession: I paid for API calls for months before discovering Hugging Face had free models that did 80% of what I needed. Always check if a local model can do the job first.

lessons.title: Build Smart Automation with AI

accomplishments: Combine automation + AI for intelligent systems

do.title: Build an AI-powered email classifier
steps: Create smart_automation.py
Copy the code below
Run it — watch AI classify emails intelligently
do.code:
from openai import OpenAI
import json, re

client = OpenAI()

def classify_email(subject, body):
    categories = ['urgent', 'support', 'sales', 'spam', 'personal']
    prompt = f"""Classify this email into ONE category: {', '.join(categories)}
Subject: {subject}
Body: {body}

Return ONLY valid JSON:
{{"category": "...", "confidence": 0.95, "action": "...", "priority": "high/medium/low"}}"""
    
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model='gpt-3.5-turbo',
        messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': prompt}],
        temperature=0.3
    )
    match = re.search(r'\{.*\}', response.choices[0].message.content, re.DOTALL)
    return json.loads(match.group()) if match else None

# Test
emails = [
    ('URGENT: Server Down', 'Production server not responding since 3am'),
    ('50% OFF SALE', 'Limited time offer on all products'),
    ('Hey!', 'Want to grab coffee this weekend?'),
]

for subject, body in emails:
    result = classify_email(subject, body)
    print(f'Subject: {subject}')
    print(f'Category: {result["category"]} | Priority: {result["priority"]}')
    print(f'Action: {result["action"]}\n')
do.result: AI classified 3 emails into categories with priority and suggested actions — automatically!
do.proofRequired: your classifier handling at least 3 different email types

concepts.name: Smart Automation
concepts.definition: Automation that makes intelligent decisions, not just follows rigid rules. Traditional: if email contains 'urgent' → flag. Smart: AI understands context, urgency, and intent.
concepts.analogy: A smart thermostat vs a manual one. The smart one learns your schedule and adjusts. The manual one just follows a timer.

concepts.name: AI + Automation Patterns
concepts.definition: Scheduled Intelligence (daily AI reports), Triggered Intelligence (AI responds to events), Intelligent Alerts (AI decides what's important), Smart Recommendations (AI suggests actions).
concepts.analogy: Different modes of a smart assistant: proactive (scheduled), reactive (triggered), advisory (alerts), predictive (recommendations).

concepts.name: Cost Optimization
concepts.definition: Cache AI responses, use local models for simple tasks, batch process instead of one-at-a-time, set token limits, use cheaper models for classification.
concepts.analogy: Bulk buying vs retail — batch your AI calls like you'd batch errands to save time and money.

understand.keyTakeaway: Smart automation = automation + AI decision-making. The system runs itself AND understands context.

build.goal: Build a smart content curator that collects, analyzes, and organizes content automatically

codeComparison.new:
import schedule, time

def smart_content_pipeline():
    # 1. Collect (API/scraping)
    articles = fetch_articles('python AI')
    # 2. Analyze (AI)
    for article in articles:
        sentiment = analyze_sentiment(article['title'])
        category = classify_content(article['summary'])
        article['ai_analysis'] = {'sentiment': sentiment, 'category': category}
    # 3. Decide (filter relevant)
    relevant = [a for a in articles if a['ai_analysis']['category'] == 'relevant']
    # 4. Act (save report)
    save_report(relevant)

schedule.every().day.at('08:00').do(smart_content_pipeline)

build.hint: The pattern is always: Collect → Analyze with AI → Decide → Act → Log.

challenge.title: Build Your Smart Automation System
challenge.mission: Create an intelligent system that combines automation, AI analysis, and smart decision-making.
challenge.starterCode:
from openai import OpenAI
import schedule, time, json

client = OpenAI()

class SmartSystem:
    def __init__(self):
        self.log = []
    
    def collect(self): pass
    def analyze(self, data): pass
    def decide(self, analysis): pass
    def act(self, decision): pass
    def run_cycle(self):
        data = self.collect()
        analysis = self.analyze(data)
        decision = self.decide(analysis)
        self.act(decision)
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a feedback loop: the system learns from past decisions to improve future ones.

lessons.devFession: I built an AI system that auto-replied to emails. It worked great... until it replied to a spam email saying 'Yes, I would like to extend my car warranty.' Always filter before you automate.

lessons.title: Build Your AI Application

accomplishments: Combine ALL skills from all 4 modules
Choose from 5 project templates or create your own

do.title: Choose and build your AI capstone project
steps: Pick a project from the 5 options (or design your own)
Plan your architecture: what classes, functions, APIs?
Build it step by step — start with the core feature
Add error handling, logging, and data persistence
Test with real data and polish
do.code:
# PROJECT OPTION 1: AI Research Assistant
from openai import OpenAI
import requests, json, os
from datetime import datetime

class AIResearchAssistant:
    def __init__(self, topic):
        self.topic = topic
        self.client = OpenAI()
        self.data_file = f'research_{topic.replace(" ", "_")}.json'
        self.findings = self.load_data()
    
    def load_data(self):
        if os.path.exists(self.data_file):
            with open(self.data_file) as f:
                return json.load(f)
        return []
    
    def save_data(self):
        with open(self.data_file, 'w') as f:
            json.dump(self.findings, f, indent=2)
    
    def search(self, query):
        # Fetch from API
        url = f'https://api.github.com/search/repositories?q={query}'
        r = requests.get(url)
        return r.json().get('items', [])[:5] if r.ok else []
    
    def summarize(self, text):
        response = self.client.chat.completions.create(
            model='gpt-3.5-turbo',
            messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': f'Summarize in 2 sentences: {text}'}]
        )
        return response.choices[0].message.content
    
    def run(self):
        print(f'AI Research Assistant: {self.topic}')
        results = self.search(self.topic)
        for r in results:
            summary = self.summarize(r.get('description', r['full_name']))
            finding = {'name': r['full_name'], 'summary': summary,
                       'stars': r['stargazers_count'], 'date': datetime.now().isoformat()}
            self.findings.append(finding)
            print(f"  {r['full_name']} ({r['stargazers_count']} stars)")
            print(f"  Summary: {summary}\n")
        self.save_data()
        print(f'Saved {len(self.findings)} findings to {self.data_file}')
do.result: A complete AI application combining APIs, AI processing, data persistence, and error handling. This is production-ready code.
do.proofRequired: your completed capstone project running with real output

understand.keyTakeaway: You just built an AI-powered application from scratch. Every skill from all 4 modules came together. You are a Python developer.
